title: Understanding Your Medicare Part D Formulary: A 2026 Guide to Prescription Drug Coverage
rawContent:
What Is a Medicare Part D Formulary?

Most people don't think twice about their prescription drug list, until they arrive at the pharmacy and learn their medication isn't covered. That moment of surprise is something millions of Medicare beneficiaries experience each year, and it's almost always preventable. Understanding your Medicare Part D formulary before you enroll is one of the most practical steps you can take to protect both your health and your wallet.

A Medicare Part D formulary is the official list of prescription drugs covered by a specific Medicare prescription drug plan. Each plan's drug list is reviewed and approved annually by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to ensure it includes a broad range of drug categories and therapeutic classes. Approval doesn't mean uniformity, though, formularies vary significantly from plan to plan, even within the same geographic region.

Two plans with nearly identical monthly premiums can cover your medications very differently. One plan might list your blood pressure medication as a preferred generic at a low copay, while another places it on a higher tier with more out-of-pocket cost. That's why side-by-side comparison matters before you commit to a plan.

As research professor Jack Hoadley, PhD, of the Georgetown University Health Policy Institute notes: "Part D formularies are reviewed and approved annually by CMS, but the complexity of tier structures and prior authorization requirements continues to create real barriers for many enrollees."

How Medicare Part D Formulary Tiers Work

Tier structure is at the heart of how Medicare prescription drug plans determine what you pay at the pharmacy. According to Kaiser Family Foundation analysis, approximately 90% of Part D plans use five or more formulary tiers. Here's how the typical structure breaks down:

  • Tier 1: Preferred generic drugs, lowest copays

  • Tier 2: Non-preferred generic drugs, slightly higher cost

  • Tier 3: Preferred brand-name drugs, moderate cost-sharing

  • Tier 4: Non-preferred brand-name drugs, higher out-of-pocket costs

  • Tier 5: Specialty drugs, highest cost-sharing, often with prior authorization

The difference between generic vs brand-name drugs on your formulary can be dramatic. A medication on Tier 1 might cost you $5 per fill, while the same drug placed on Tier 4 by a different plan could cost $80 or more. Private plan sponsors, including Blue MedicareRx plans and other insurers, design their own tier structures within CMS guidelines, so the same brand-name drug can sit on different tiers across competing plans.

Juliette Cubanski, PhD, Associate Director of the Program on Medicare Policy at KFF, puts it plainly: "Formulary design is one of the most powerful tools insurers have to manage drug costs, and beneficiaries often don't realize until they fill a prescription that their drug is on a more expensive tier, or not covered at all."

2026 Medicare Part D Costs You Need to Know

Before comparing plans, it helps to understand the financial framework for Medicare drug coverage 2026. Here are the key benchmarks set by CMS:

Cost Component

2025 Amount

2026 Amount

Average Monthly Premium

$34.50

Maximum Annual Deductible

$590

$615

Catastrophic Coverage Threshold

$2,000

$2,100

Late Enrollment Penalty Base

$38.99

$38.99

Consider a real-world example: a beneficiary taking a Tier 4 brand-name drug for a chronic condition pays the $615 deductible first, then their plan's coinsurance rate on each refill. If their total drug costs climb to $2,100, they enter the catastrophic phase, after which their cost-sharing drops significantly. Understanding this arc helps you estimate your true annual drug spending, not just your monthly premium.

Higher-income beneficiaries also face an IRMAA Part D surcharge ranging from $14. 50 to $91. 00 per month on top of their plan's premium, based on 2024 income tax returns.

Individual income thresholds begin at $109,000. And if you delay enrolling in Part D without qualifying coverage elsewhere, the late enrollment penalty base of $38. 99 applies permanently, another reason to review Medicare Part D late enrollment penalties carefully.

How to Find Out If Your Drug Is Covered

The most reliable way to verify drug coverage is to use Medicare's Plan Finder tool at Medicare.gov. Enter each of your current prescriptions by name, dosage, and frequency, and the tool shows you how each available plan covers those specific drugs, including tier placement, prior authorization requirements, and quantity limits.

You can also request the drug list Medicare plans publish directly from any insurer. These formularies are posted on plan websites and available in print on request. When reviewing them, look beyond whether a drug is listed, check for step therapy requirements (where you must try a cheaper drug first) and any coverage restrictions that could affect your access.

Over 50 million Medicare beneficiaries are currently enrolled in Part D plans, according to Kaiser Family Foundation data from 2025. Yet many do not check their formulary during each open enrollment season. That oversight matters, because drug coverage, and your costs, can change from year to year in ways that add up quickly.

For a broader understanding of what Part D covers and how it fits into your overall benefits, visit our overview of Medicare Part D plans and coverage rules.

When and How Part D Formularies Can Change

Formularies are updated at the start of each new plan year, meaning a drug you relied on in 2025 could move to a higher tier, require new prior authorization, or disappear from your plan's coverage entirely in 2026. This is why the Annual Notice of Change (ANOC) mailed each fall, deserves careful attention rather than a quick trip to the recycling bin.

Mid-year changes are also possible. Plans can add prior authorization requirements to a drug during the year, though CMS rules generally protect beneficiaries who are already taking an affected medication. If you're currently using a drug and your plan makes a mid-year change, your plan is typically required to provide a transition fill, a temporary supply while you work with your doctor on an alternative or file an exception request.

Tricia Neuman, ScD, Senior Vice President and Executive Director of Medicare Policy at KFF, highlights why this matters: "The structure of Part D formularies has a direct impact on access and affordability for millions of Medicare beneficiaries, particularly those with complex or chronic conditions who depend on multiple medications."

What to Do If Your Drug Is Not on the Formulary

Finding out your medication isn't covered doesn't have to mean going without it. You have three primary options:

  1. Request a formulary exception your prescribing doctor submits documentation of medical necessity to your plan

  2. Ask about a covered therapeutic alternative a different drug in the same class that your plan does cover

  3. File a formal appeal if your exception request is denied, you can escalate through the Medicare appeals process

For formulary exceptions, plans must respond within 72 hours of receiving a complete request. When your health is at immediate risk, you can request an expedited review and the plan must respond within 24 hours. If the exception is denied, you can request a redetermination, then reconsideration by an Independent Review Entity, and eventually escalation to an Administrative Law Judge. Each level has defined timeframes, so keep records and track deadlines.

David Lipschutz, JD, Associate Director and Senior Policy Attorney at the Medicare Rights Center, captures why awareness matters here: "Beneficiaries have the right to request exceptions to a formulary decision, but most don't know this option exists, understanding that right can mean the difference between affordable care and going without a needed medication."

Extra Help: Reducing Your Part D Drug Costs

The Extra Help program, also called the Low-Income Subsidy, is one of the most powerful and underused tools in Medicare. It can substantially lower your Part D premiums, reduce or eliminate the annual deductible, and cut your copays for both generic and brand-name drugs.

For 2025, eligibility thresholds are:

  • Individual income limit: $23,475

  • Married couple income limit: $31,725

  • Individual resource limit: $17,600

  • Married couple resource limit: $35,130

Qualifying beneficiaries may see the $615 deductible reduced or eliminated entirely, and copays for covered drugs drop to just a few dollars per fill. Applying is free and has no effect on your other Medicare benefits. You can check eligibility and apply through Medicare. gov or through the National Council on Aging's BenefitsCheckUp tool. The

medicarefaq. com/faqs/medicare-extra-help-program/">Medicare Extra Help program page has detailed information on how to get started.

How to Choose the Right Part D Plan for Your Prescriptions

Choosing a Part D plan benefits program based solely on the monthly premium is one of the most common, and costly, mistakes Medicare beneficiaries make. A plan with a $0 premium may place your specific medications on Tier 4, resulting in far higher annual costs than a plan with the $34.50 average premium and preferred tier coverage for those same drugs.

Use this practical checklist before open enrollment ends each October 15 – December 7:

  • List every prescription medication you currently take, including dosage and frequency

  • Enter all drugs into Medicare's Plan Finder to see tier placement across candidate plans

  • Compare total estimated annual costs, not just monthly premiums

  • Check for prior authorization and step therapy requirements on each drug

  • Verify whether your preferred pharmacy is in-network for each plan

Also consider the difference between standalone Medicare prescription drug plans and Medicare Advantage plans with built-in drug coverage. Formularies differ between these two plan types, and the right choice depends on your full healthcare picture, not just your drug list. A licensed Medicare agent can compare top Medicare prescription drug plans side by side for your ZIP code, including Blue MedicareRx plans and other regional options, to find the best fit for your medications and budget.

Frequently Asked Questions About Medicare Part D Formularies

What is a Medicare Part D formulary?

A Medicare Part D formulary is the official list of prescription drugs covered by a specific Part D plan, organized into tiers that determine your out-of-pocket cost at the pharmacy. Each plan's formulary is approved annually by CMS, and the drug list can differ significantly between plans, even those available in the same area with similar premiums.

How do I find out if my drug is covered by my Part D plan?

Use Medicare's Plan Finder at Medicare.gov to search by drug name and compare coverage across available plans, including tier placement and any prior authorization requirements. You can also call your current plan directly to ask about your specific medication's coverage status and cost-sharing for 2026.

What are the tiers in a Medicare Part D formulary?

Most Part D plans use a five-tier structure ranging from preferred generics (lowest cost) to specialty drugs (highest cost). Generic vs brand-name drugs are placed on different tiers, with generics generally costing far less, and specialty medications often requiring higher cost-sharing or prior authorization before the plan will cover them.

What can I do if my drug is not on my Part D formulary?

You can request a formulary exception with documentation from your prescribing doctor, ask about a covered therapeutic alternative, or file a formal appeal if the exception is denied. Expedited exception requests, available when a delay could harm your health, must receive a plan response within 24 hours.

Can my Part D plan change its formulary mid-year?

Plans can make certain formulary changes during the year, but CMS rules require advance notice and a transition drug supply for beneficiaries already taking an affected medication. Reviewing your Annual Notice of Change each fall remains the best way to spot upcoming formulary changes before they take effect on January 1.

Your prescriptions are too important to leave to chance during open enrollment. Taking time to verify your drugs on your plan's formulary, and understanding your rights when coverage falls short, puts you in a much stronger position heading into 2026. If you want personalized help comparing your options, speak with a licensed Medicare agent near you who can walk through the details specific to your medications and location.
excerpt: A Medicare Part D formulary is the list of drugs covered by your plan. Understanding how formularies and their tier structures work is crucial for managing prescription costs.
author: David Haass
keyTakeaways: A Medicare Part D formulary is the official list of prescription drugs covered by a specific plan, reviewed and approved annually by CMS.
Formularies vary significantly between plans, even with similar premiums, impacting your out-of-pocket costs for medications.
Most Part D plans use a tier structure (e.g., 5 tiers) where lower tiers (e.g., preferred generics) have lower copays than higher tiers (e.g., specialty drugs).
The placement of a drug on a specific tier directly determines your cost-sharing, making side-by-side plan comparison essential.
Private plan sponsors design their own tier structures within CMS guidelines, meaning the same drug can be on different tiers across competing plans.
